mappings.rationaleAI: Covers the founding of the Tang dynasty by the Li family, the period of Tang power under Taizong (626–649), and the An Lushan rebellion (755), which marks the precise end-point of section 931. Administrative, cultural, and military developments under the early Tang map directly to D.2.c, D.2.i, and D.2.i.ii.

The Silk Road was established during Han expansion into Central Asia under Wu Ti, directly relevant to C.2.i.ii (expansion into Central Asia), and supported Han cultural life including trade and foreign contacts at C.2.i.iii.

Covers the unification of the Warring States by the Ch'in in 221 BC, the abolition of feudalism, the Great Wall's construction, Legalist governance under Li Ssu, and the fall of the dynasty — the precise content of C.1, C.1.a, and C.1.b.

Comprehensively covers the entire Han dynasty arc: Western Han establishment by Liu Pang, expansion under Wu Ti into Central Asia, the Wang Mang usurpation, Eastern Han restoration, and cultural attainments including papermaking and introduction of Buddhism — mapping to all sub-paths of C.2.

Provides broad coverage of East Asian ancient history within its global survey of writing-era civilizations, encompassing Chinese Bronze Age states and classical dynasties that correspond to sections B and C of the outline.

Covers the post-classical period from 500 CE onward including the Tang dynasty's role in Silk Road trade, Buddhist transmission, and the Six Dynasties transition — relevant to the D paths on the breakdown and revival of the Chinese empire.

The general China article spans all periods covered by section 931 — from Paleolithic habitation and Yellow River dynastic states through the Qin, Han, and Tang — providing broad coverage relevant to the section's overview node A and all major dynastic phases.

Confucius lived in the Spring and Autumn period (c. 551–479 BC) and his teachings are the centerpiece of the 'Classical period of Chinese literature and philosophy' at B.2.e, which explicitly names Confucianism as one of the 'hundred schools.'
Laozi is the founding figure of Taoism, named in B.2.e as part of the Classical philosophical period alongside Confucianism; Taoist resurgence during the Six Dynasties period maps to D.1.b.

Taoism is explicitly included in B.2.e as one of the Classical Chinese philosophical and religious traditions (Chuang-tzu, the Naturalists), and its resurgence during the Six Dynasties period of intellectual change is the subject of D.1.b.
Covers Confucianism, Taoism, Legalism, and Mohism — all named in B.2.e as the 'hundred schools' of Classical Chinese philosophy — and places them within the Warring States intellectual context.

Covers the Paleolithic, Mesolithic, and Neolithic periods globally including in East Asia, mapping to B.1.a (Paleolithic and Mesolithic stages in North China, Ordos industries) and B.1.b (Neolithic pebble tools and domesticated animals).

B.1.d specifically references the Early Bronze Age in North China and bronze objects in the Ordos region as part of the Late Neolithic/Lung-shan complex; the Bronze Age article covers the East Asian regional Bronze Age chronology.

The Iron Age spread into East Asia during the Eastern Chou and Warring States period, directly relevant to B.2.d and B.2.i.ii covering urbanization, economic development, and material culture change in that era.

Explicitly traces Chinese history from Bronze Age Zhou and Qin through the Han and Tang dynasties, covering the same geographic and chronological scope as the full section 931 outline including overview context for A.

Survey of human development explicitly covers early Chinese civilizations and the Tang/Song dynasties among Axial Age transitions, providing broad background coverage for sections B and C on the emergence and unification of Chinese civilization.
